does: This paper introduces Portable Agent Memory, a protocol enabling persistent context transfer across different AI agents and model architectures. The core contribution is a structured memory system with five components: content-addressable entries organized through a Merkle-DAG provenance graph for tamper-detection, capability-based access control for selective memory disclosure, an injection-resistant rehydration protocol that adapts recalled memories to heterogeneous target models, and a JSON/CBOR serialization format for efficient transport. The authors provide a Python SDK with 54 passing tests and demonstrate successful memory transfer between major models including GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, and Llama, validating cross-model compatibility and the practical feasibility of their approach.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental limitation in current AI agent ecosystems where context accumulated during agent operation remains siloed within vendor-specific systems. It extends prior research on agent memory architectures and knowledge representation by adding cryptographic verification and portability guarantees. The approach builds on established concepts in content-addressable storage and Merkle-DAGs from distributed systems, while combining these with capability-based security models from systems security. The injection-resistant rehydration protocol responds to recent concerns about prompt injection attacks in agent systems, attempting to safely bridge semantic gaps when transferring memories between architecturally different models with varying tokenization and reasoning patterns.
enables: This protocol opens pathways toward truly portable AI agent ecosystems where users own and control their agent memories rather than having them locked into vendor platforms. It enables downstream work on agent memory marketplaces, portable skill libraries, and interoperable multi-agent systems where agents can collaborate and transfer learned knowledge. The cryptographic verification layer supports auditability and regulatory compliance use cases in agent deployments. Future work could extend this toward standardized memory formats across the AI industry, federated agent networks sharing curated memory graphs, and advanced techniques for memory compression and selective forgetting. The open protocol foundation also enables the research community to study how heterogeneous architectures process and utilize transferred episodic, semantic, and procedural knowledge.
